MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates its proposed Sentence Encoder against concrete baselines (Random and Unigram) using the same Brown corpus and CMU dictionary and measures memorability with immediate and 7-day recall plus recognition, which supports fair within-experiment comparisons. However, important stronger or more recent baselines (e.g., neural language-model based generation or direct comparisons to existing mnemonic-generation tools) appear absent, and the user-study sample reduction (167→66) raises power concerns for claimed advantages. The authors do acknowledge limitations of prior tools and analyze differences (POS templates, n-gram scoring, post-processing), and citation coverage is described as adequate, so the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper evaluates its Sentence Encoder against clear baselines (Random and Unigram) using the same Brown-corpus-derived models and identical memorability metrics, and it explicitly situates itself relative to prior online mnemonic tools. Comparisons appear fair and the authors acknowledge limitations where other methods might match performance (and discuss shortcomings like POS encoder lacking verbs). However, from the summary it omits stronger contemporary baselines (e.g., neural language models or existing full-sentence mnemonic systems) and practical tool comparisons, so the experimental coverage is good but not fully comprehensive for deployment-relevant claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates its proposed Sentence Encoder against concrete baselines (Random and Unigram) using the same Brown corpus and CMU dictionary and measures memorability with immediate and 7-day recall plus recognition, which supports fair within-experiment comparisons. However, important stronger or more recent baselines (e.g., neural language-model based generation or direct comparisons to existing mnemonic-generation tools) appear absent, and the user-study sample reduction (167→66) raises power concerns for claimed advantages. The authors do acknowledge limitations of prior tools and analyze differences (POS templates, n-gram scoring, post-processing), and citation coverage is described as adequate, so the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates its Sentence Encoder against clear baselines (Random and Unigram) using the same Brown-corpus-derived models and identical memorability metrics, and it explicitly situates itself relative to prior online mnemonic tools. Comparisons appear fair and the authors acknowledge limitations where other methods might match performance (and discuss shortcomings like POS encoder lacking verbs). However, from the summary it omits stronger contemporary baselines (e.g., neural language models or existing full-sentence mnemonic systems) and practical tool comparisons, so the experimental coverage is good but not fully comprehensive for deployment-relevant claims.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): This paper tackles a practical and under-explored problem (automatic sentence-based mnemonic generation) and provides an end-to-end method plus code and a user study showing the Sentence Encoder improves memorability over simple baselines. From an experimentalist perspective the work is useful and reproducible (Brown corpus, CMU dict, hyperparameters reported), so it can influence applied work in password/memory aids. However impact is constrained by classical n‑gram/POS modelling, limited usable user study size (66 participants after fraud removal), and some admitted generation limitations (POS lacks verbs, chunk encoder is slow), so it is a moderate rather than transformative contribution. | B(3): This paper tackles a practical usability problem—automatically generating sentence-based mnemonic encodings for numbers—and demonstrates through a user study that a POS-template + n-gram Sentence Encoder yields measurably more memorable representations than simple baselines. The approach is grounded in standard resources (Brown corpus, CMU dictionary) and produces usable code, making it likely to be adopted or extended by researchers and practitioners working on password and memory aids. However, limitations such as reduced usable sample size in the user study, some syntactic weaknesses (e.g., lack of verbs), and reliance on relatively small corpora constrain immediate broad impact; the work is a useful, incremental step that opens plausible follow-on work rather than a transformative shift.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles a practical and under-explored problem (automatic sentence-based mnemonic generation) and provides an end-to-end method plus code and a user study showing the Sentence Encoder improves memorability over simple baselines. From an experimentalist perspective the work is useful and reproducible (Brown corpus, CMU dict, hyperparameters reported), so it can influence applied work in password/memory aids. However impact is constrained by classical n‑gram/POS modelling, limited usable user study size (66 participants after fraud removal), and some admitted generation limitations (POS lacks verbs, chunk encoder is slow), so it is a moderate rather than transformative contribution.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles a practical usability problem—automatically generating sentence-based mnemonic encodings for numbers—and demonstrates through a user study that a POS-template + n-gram Sentence Encoder yields measurably more memorable representations than simple baselines. The approach is grounded in standard resources (Brown corpus, CMU dictionary) and produces usable code, making it likely to be adopted or extended by researchers and practitioners working on password and memory aids. However, limitations such as reduced usable sample size in the user study, some syntactic weaknesses (e.g., lack of verbs), and reliance on relatively small corpora constrain immediate broad impact; the work is a useful, incremental step that opens plausible follow-on work rather than a transformative shift.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well-organized with a clear flow from introduction through methods, experiments, and conclusions, and the main contributions (automatic mnemonic sentence generation and the Sentence Encoder model) are stated early. Notation and methodological descriptions (n-gram/POS models, CMU dictionary filtering, scoring and post-processing) are described clearly enough to follow, and writing quality is reported as good. A few presentation gaps remain—e.g., discussion of the POS encoder’s verb omission and the user-study fraud issue could be more precisely integrated into the results/limitations, and some examples or figure references could better highlight differences between encoders—but overall the paper communicates its ideas effectively. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from motivation through methods and experiments, and the main contributions (automatic sentence-based mnemonics and the Sentence Encoder architecture) are stated early. Notation and methodological details (training corpora, CMU dictionary use, hyperparameters) are reported consistently, and writing quality is generally good with adequate related-work coverage. Figures, tables, and examples appear effective in illustrating comparisons between encoders, though a few presentation issues remain (e.g., discussion of POS encoder drawbacks and the reduction in usable study participants due to fraudulent responses could be clarified).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well-organized with a clear flow from introduction through methods, experiments, and conclusions, and the main contributions (automatic mnemonic sentence generation and the Sentence Encoder model) are stated early. Notation and methodological descriptions (n-gram/POS models, CMU dictionary filtering, scoring and post-processing) are described clearly enough to follow, and writing quality is reported as good. A few presentation gaps remain—e.g., discussion of the POS encoder’s verb omission and the user-study fraud issue could be more precisely integrated into the results/limitations, and some examples or figure references could better highlight differences between encoders—but overall the paper communicates its ideas effectively.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from motivation through methods and experiments, and the main contributions (automatic sentence-based mnemonics and the Sentence Encoder architecture) are stated early. Notation and methodological details (training corpora, CMU dictionary use, hyperparameters) are reported consistently, and writing quality is generally good with adequate related-work coverage. Figures, tables, and examples appear effective in illustrating comparisons between encoders, though a few presentation issues remain (e.g., discussion of POS encoder drawbacks and the reduction in usable study participants due to fraudulent responses could be clarified).

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper states clear claims and presents an end-to-end system plus a user study showing the Sentence Encoder outperforms baselines; code and hyperparameters are provided which supports reproducibility. However, the experimental evidence has weaknesses: the usable sample size was reduced dramatically (167 → 66) due to fraudulent responses, and the summary does not report power analysis or detailed statistical tests/effect sizes to confirm significance. Methodology otherwise appears sound with reasonable baselines and ablations (n-gram/POS models, CMU lexicon), but I would have expected more rigorous statistical reporting and controls to fully rule out confounds before treating the results as definitive. | B(4): The paper states clear empirical claims and supports them with a comparative user study showing the Sentence Encoder outperforms baselines; models, datasets (Brown corpus, CMUdict), and hyperparameters are reported. There are minor technical concerns: the usable study sample dropped from 167 to 66 due to fraudulent responses which raises statistical-power and ecological-validity questions, and no formal statistical-test details are reported in the summary. No mathematical derivations were required and none are missing; engineering limitations (POS sentences lacking verbs, slow Chunk encoder) are acknowledged. Overall the methods and evidence are sound but the reduced and possibly underpowered user study and limited reporting of statistical analysis are the main issues.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and presents an end-to-end system plus a user study showing the Sentence Encoder outperforms baselines; code and hyperparameters are provided which supports reproducibility. However, the experimental evidence has weaknesses: the usable sample size was reduced dramatically (167 → 66) due to fraudulent responses, and the summary does not report power analysis or detailed statistical tests/effect sizes to confirm significance. Methodology otherwise appears sound with reasonable baselines and ablations (n-gram/POS models, CMU lexicon), but I would have expected more rigorous statistical reporting and controls to fully rule out confounds before treating the results as definitive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and supports them with a comparative user study showing the Sentence Encoder outperforms baselines; models, datasets (Brown corpus, CMUdict), and hyperparameters are reported. There are minor technical concerns: the usable study sample dropped from 167 to 66 due to fraudulent responses which raises statistical-power and ecological-validity questions, and no formal statistical-test details are reported in the summary. No mathematical derivations were required and none are missing; engineering limitations (POS sentences lacking verbs, slow Chunk encoder) are acknowledged. Overall the methods and evidence are sound but the reduced and possibly underpowered user study and limited reporting of statistical analysis are the main issues.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper sits well within the NLP domain: it applies standard language-modeling techniques (n-gram, POS templates), uses canonical resources (Brown corpus, CMU dict), and includes code and hyperparameters for reproducibility. The human-subject evaluation of memorability (immediate and 7-day recall, recognition, rankings) targets the right audience for applied NLP work, though the relatively narrow application (numeric mnemonics) and modest usable sample size (66 after fraud filtering) limit broad ML impact. Overall the methods and evaluation meet venue expectations, but scope is somewhat specialized, making it a good (but not perfect) fit for a top-tier NLP/ML conference. | B(4): This paper sits squarely in applied NLP, tackling a human-facing language generation problem (mnemonic sentence encoding of numbers) and using standard resources (Brown corpus, CMU dictionary) plus human-subject evaluation—elements expected at a top venue. The methods (n-gram/POS templates) are not cutting-edge ML, so novelty is modest, but the combination of generation, careful scoring, and ecologically-relevant memorability study addresses an important usability/security problem that the community should see. Minor scope concerns stem from relatively simple modeling choices and user-study limitations (fraudulent responses, sample size), but the work is well-positioned for NLP/ML audiences focused on human-centered language technology.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper sits well within the NLP domain: it applies standard language-modeling techniques (n-gram, POS templates), uses canonical resources (Brown corpus, CMU dict), and includes code and hyperparameters for reproducibility. The human-subject evaluation of memorability (immediate and 7-day recall, recognition, rankings) targets the right audience for applied NLP work, though the relatively narrow application (numeric mnemonics) and modest usable sample size (66 after fraud filtering) limit broad ML impact. Overall the methods and evaluation meet venue expectations, but scope is somewhat specialized, making it a good (but not perfect) fit for a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely in applied NLP, tackling a human-facing language generation problem (mnemonic sentence encoding of numbers) and using standard resources (Brown corpus, CMU dictionary) plus human-subject evaluation—elements expected at a top venue. The methods (n-gram/POS templates) are not cutting-edge ML, so novelty is modest, but the combination of generation, careful scoring, and ecologically-relevant memorability study addresses an important usability/security problem that the community should see. Minor scope concerns stem from relatively simple modeling choices and user-study limitations (fraudulent responses, sample size), but the work is well-positioned for NLP/ML audiences focused on human-centered language technology.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies an established mnemonic scheme (the major system) but contributes a new practical pipeline that combines POS sentence templates with an n‑gram language model and CMU lexicon constraints to generate ranked sentence encodings. This is a meaningful engineering advance over one‑ or two‑word online tools and is empirically validated in a user study, yet the core methods (n‑grams, POS templates, CMU dictionary) are existing techniques rather than fundamentally new algorithms. From an expert perspective the contribution is a solid, moderately novel application and integration rather than a highly original theoretical advance. | B(3): The paper presents a modest but clear extension of existing mnemonic/password encoding work by combining POS-based sentence templates with an n-gram language model (trained on the Brown corpus) and CMU pronunciation constraints to generate ranked sentence mnemonics for the Major system. This is not a radically new problem formulation—mnemonic encodings and sentence generation are well-studied—but the particular pipeline (POS templates + trigram scoring + digit-count weighting) is a non-trivial, practically useful engineering contribution that is differentiated from prior one- or two-word tools. From my applied usable-security perspective the methods would likely be new to practitioners but would not surprise a language-modeling expert, hence a moderate originality rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies an established mnemonic scheme (the major system) but contributes a new practical pipeline that combines POS sentence templates with an n‑gram language model and CMU lexicon constraints to generate ranked sentence encodings. This is a meaningful engineering advance over one‑ or two‑word online tools and is empirically validated in a user study, yet the core methods (n‑grams, POS templates, CMU dictionary) are existing techniques rather than fundamentally new algorithms. From an expert perspective the contribution is a solid, moderately novel application and integration rather than a highly original theoretical advance.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a modest but clear extension of existing mnemonic/password encoding work by combining POS-based sentence templates with an n-gram language model (trained on the Brown corpus) and CMU pronunciation constraints to generate ranked sentence mnemonics for the Major system. This is not a radically new problem formulation—mnemonic encodings and sentence generation are well-studied—but the particular pipeline (POS templates + trigram scoring + digit-count weighting) is a non-trivial, practically useful engineering contribution that is differentiated from prior one- or two-word tools. From my applied usable-security perspective the methods would likely be new to practitioners but would not surprise a language-modeling expert, hence a moderate originality rating.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper offers a solid experimental evaluation: multiple encoder variants (including Random and Unigram baselines), immediate and 7‑day recall, recognition tests, and participant rankings, and it reports hyperparameters and releases code and used corpora (Brown, CMUdict). However, the user‑study evidence is weakened by a substantial reduction in usable participants (167→66) due to fraudulent responses, and the paper appears to lack thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses exploring key design choices (e.g., n‑gram order, scoring weights, template selection). Related work is covered and several failure modes and limitations (POS encoder verb paucity, Chunk encoder runtime) are discussed, supporting reproducibility but leaving some experimental gaps for full confidence. | B(4): The paper presents a solid experimental evaluation (immediate and 7-day recall, recognition, ranked memorability, timing) and compares multiple encoder variants (Random, Unigram, POS/Chunk, Sentence Encoder), with code and hyperparameters available to support reproducibility. Related work coverage is adequate and several limitations are candidly discussed (POS encoder issues, slow Chunk parsing, and fraudulent study responses reducing N). However, the study’s ecological validity is weakened by the reduced usable sample size and I see no evidence of thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., impact of digit-count weighting, template selection, corpus choice), which would be important to fully support the claimed superiority of the Sentence Encoder. Overall the experiments are strong but miss some analyses that would make the evidence comprehensive for deployment decisions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper offers a solid experimental evaluation: multiple encoder variants (including Random and Unigram baselines), immediate and 7‑day recall, recognition tests, and participant rankings, and it reports hyperparameters and releases code and used corpora (Brown, CMUdict). However, the user‑study evidence is weakened by a substantial reduction in usable participants (167→66) due to fraudulent responses, and the paper appears to lack thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses exploring key design choices (e.g., n‑gram order, scoring weights, template selection). Related work is covered and several failure modes and limitations (POS encoder verb paucity, Chunk encoder runtime) are discussed, supporting reproducibility but leaving some experimental gaps for full confidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a solid experimental evaluation (immediate and 7-day recall, recognition, ranked memorability, timing) and compares multiple encoder variants (Random, Unigram, POS/Chunk, Sentence Encoder), with code and hyperparameters available to support reproducibility. Related work coverage is adequate and several limitations are candidly discussed (POS encoder issues, slow Chunk parsing, and fraudulent study responses reducing N). However, the study’s ecological validity is weakened by the reduced usable sample size and I see no evidence of thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., impact of digit-count weighting, template selection, corpus choice), which would be important to fully support the claimed superiority of the Sentence Encoder. Overall the experiments are strong but miss some analyses that would make the evidence comprehensive for deployment decisions.

main_claims: We present a method to automatically generate memorable sentence-based mnemonic encodings of numbers using the major system.
We propose several encoding models (including baselines and a final Sentence Encoder) and evaluate them in a user study of memorability.
A model combining part-of-speech sentence templates with an n-gram language model (the Sentence Encoder) produces the most memorable password representations.
_summary.methodology: Train n-gram and POS models on the Brown corpus and use the CMU Pronouncing Dictionary to ensure pronunciable words; generate encodings by sampling POS sentence templates and selecting words that encode digits while scoring with a trigram model (with digit-count weighting and post-processing).
metrics: response time / time spent on tasks
limitations: Many online tools only produce one- or two-word encodings and do not produce ranked sentence outputs (limitation of prior tools, discussed).
POS encoder tends to lack verbs in generated sentences (stated drawback).
Chunk encoder has slow running time due to expensive parsing of noun/verb phrases (stated drawback).
User study experienced fraudulent responses which reduced usable sample size (from 167 to 66 participants).

reviewer_a.name: Rigorous Experimentalist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer and NLP experimentalist with extensive experience designing reproducible user studies and building evaluation pipelines for language-generation systems.
traits: looks for clear baselines, ablation studies, and controls to support claims
values statistical rigor: appropriate significance testing, effect sizes, and power analysis
expects reproducibility: hyperparameters, datasets, seed control, and implementation details
checks for evaluation validity: metric choices, possible confounds, and fair comparisons
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on the experimental evidence and reproducibility: will interrogate methodology, data, and statistical claims and request additional analyses or documentation needed to support the conclusions.

reviewer_b.name: Applied Security & Usability Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher at the intersection of usable security and NLP, with a track record of evaluating mnemonic techniques, password usability, and the real-world deployment implications of human-facing language models.
traits: assesses novelty and positioning relative to prior mnemonic and password-memorability work
prioritizes real-world impact: usability, threat model relevance, and deployment constraints
attends to user study ecological validity: participant population, task realism, and long-term memorability
considers ethical and security trade-offs, including potential misuse and privacy concerns
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper by situating contributions within prior work and practical needs, emphasizing whether the proposed method meaningfully advances usability or security and what additional evidence would convince practitioners.
